hint: μ = exp(η); with a log link exp(β) is a multiplicative rate ratio.
front:
A Poisson GLM with log link gives η = −0.5 + 0.3·x₁ + 0.8·x₂, where x₁ is numeric and x₂ is a 0/1 indicator.

(i) Estimate the expected count when x₁ = 2 and x₂ = 1. **[2]**
(ii) Interpret the coefficient 0.3. **[1]**
(iii) Interpret the coefficient 0.8. **[1]**
(iv) State two features of the Poisson distribution that make it suitable for counts. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** μ = exp(η); with a log link exp(β) is a multiplicative rate ratio.

**Solution:**
(i) η = −0.5 + 0.3·2 + 0.8·1 = −0.5 + 0.6 + 0.8 = 0.9; μ = e^0.9 = 2.46.
(ii) exp(0.3) = 1.35: each one-unit increase in x₁ multiplies the expected count by 1.35 (a 35% increase), holding x₂ fixed.
(iii) exp(0.8) = 2.23: the group with x₂ = 1 has 2.23 times the expected count of the baseline (x₂ = 0).
(iv) It models non-negative integer counts, and it has mean = variance (a single parameter λ).

mark_scheme.point: Two valid Poisson features (counts; mean = variance)

hint: π = e^η/(1+e^η); exp(β) is an odds ratio; for a c-unit change use exp(cβ).
front:
A logistic regression gives logit(π) = −3.0 + 0.05·age + 1.2·smoker.

(i) Calculate the claim probability for a 40-year-old smoker. **[3]**
(ii) Interpret the smoker coefficient as an odds ratio. **[2]**
(iii) Calculate the odds ratio for a 10-year increase in age. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** π = e^η/(1+e^η); exp(β) is an odds ratio; for a c-unit change use exp(cβ).

**Solution:**
(i) η = −3.0 + 0.05·40 + 1.2·1 = −3.0 + 2.0 + 1.2 = 0.2; π = e^0.2/(1+e^0.2) = 1.221/2.221 = 0.550.
(ii) exp(1.2) = 3.32: smokers have 3.32 times the odds of a claim compared with non-smokers of the same age.
(iii) exp(0.05·10) = exp(0.5) = 1.65: each extra 10 years of age multiplies the odds of a claim by 1.65.

hint: AIC = −2 ln L + 2k, lower is better; valid for non-nested models too.
front:
Two GLMs for claim severity are compared: Model A has AIC 520.3, Model B has AIC 512.8.

(i) State what AIC measures and its formula. **[2]**
(ii) State which model is preferred. **[1]**
(iii) Explain why AIC (rather than a deviance difference) is used when models are not nested. **[2]**
(iv) Give one reason a Gamma distribution suits claim severity. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** AIC = −2·log-likelihood + 2k (k = number of parameters); lower is better; it applies to any models, nested or not.

**Solution:**
(i) AIC = −2 ln L + 2k: it trades off goodness of fit (the maximised log-likelihood) against complexity (the number of parameters k).
(ii) Model B is preferred (lower AIC, 512.8 < 520.3).
(iii) A deviance/χ² difference test is only valid for nested models (one a restriction of the other); AIC can compare non-nested models, penalising extra parameters directly.
(iv) Claim severities are continuous, strictly positive and right-skewed, which the Gamma distribution captures.

mark_scheme.point: Deviance test needs nested models; AIC does not

hint: PI = ŷ ± t_{0.025,n−2}·se(prediction); wider than the CI for the mean.
front:
A fitted regression predicts ŷ = 25.0 at x = x₀, with standard error of prediction (for a new observation) 2.0, on 18 degrees of freedom.

(i) State how the prediction interval differs from the confidence interval for the mean. **[1]**
(ii) Calculate the 95% prediction interval for a new observation at x₀. **[3]**
(iii) Explain why the prediction interval is the wider of the two. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** PI = ŷ ± t_{0.025,n−2}·se(prediction); the prediction se includes the variance of a single new point.

**Solution:**
(i) The confidence interval estimates the mean response at x₀; the prediction interval covers an individual new observation, so it also includes that observation's own random variation.
(ii) t_{0.025,18} = 2.101. PI = 25.0 ± 2.101·2.0 = 25.0 ± 4.20 = (20.8, 29.2).
(iii) It is wider because it combines the uncertainty in the estimated mean with the irreducible variability of an individual observation about that mean.

mark_scheme.point: PI covers an individual; CI covers the mean

hint: SST = SSR + SSE; R² = SSR/SST; σ̂² = SSE/(n−2); F = MSR/MSE.
front:
A simple linear regression on n = 22 observations has regression sum of squares SSR = 240 and error sum of squares SSE = 60.

(i) Calculate R². **[1]**
(ii) Calculate the residual standard error. **[2]**
(iii) Test the regression for significance at the 5% level. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** SST = SSR + SSE; R² = SSR/SST; σ̂² = SSE/(n−2); F = MSR/MSE on (1, n−2) df.

**Solution:**
(i) SST = 240 + 60 = 300; R² = 240/300 = 0.80.
(ii) σ̂² = SSE/(n−2) = 60/20 = 3; residual standard error = √3 = 1.73.
(iii) F = MSR/MSE = (240/1)/(60/20) = 240/3 = 80 on (1, 20) df. F_{0.05,1,20} = 4.35; since 80 > 4.35, the regression is highly significant.

hint: F = larger/smaller variance; if unequal, use the Welch t-test (no pooling, adjusted df).
front:
Two samples have variances s₁² = 200 (n₁ = 10) and s₂² = 20 (n₂ = 10).

(i) Test whether the population variances are equal at the 5% level. **[3]**
(ii) Hence state which two-sample t-test you should use. **[1]**
(iii) State one way that test differs from the pooled t-test. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** F = larger/smaller sample variance ~ F(df₁, df₂); if variances differ, use the Welch (unequal-variance) t-test.

**Solution:**
(i) H₀: σ₁² = σ₂². F = 200/20 = 10 on (9, 9) df. Two-sided 5% critical value F_{0.025,9,9} = 4.03. Since 10 > 4.03, reject H₀: the variances differ.
(ii) Use the Welch (unequal-variance) two-sample t-test.
(iii) Welch does not pool the variances (it keeps them separate) and uses a reduced, adjusted degrees of freedom instead of n₁ + n₂ − 2.

mark_scheme.point: Welch keeps variances separate / adjusts df

hint: Fit λ from the sample mean; expected = n·P(X=k); df reduced by 1 for the estimated λ.
front:
The number of claims per policy is recorded for 100 policies: 0 claims ×50, 1 ×30, 2 ×15, 3+ ×5. Test whether a Poisson distribution fits, at the 5% level.

(i) Estimate the Poisson mean λ. **[1]**
(ii) Calculate the expected frequencies. **[2]**
(iii) Calculate the chi-square statistic. **[2]**
(iv) State the degrees of freedom (noting the estimated parameter) and conclude. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** Estimate λ from the sample mean, get expected = n·P(X=k) under the Poisson, χ² = Σ(O−E)²/E, and reduce the df by 1 for the estimated λ.

**Solution:**
(i) λ̂ = (0·50 + 1·30 + 2·15 + 3·5)/100 = 75/100 = 0.75.
(ii) With λ = 0.75: P(0) = e^(−0.75) = 0.472, P(1) = 0.354, P(2) = 0.133, P(≥3) = 0.040. Expected (×100): 47.2, 35.4, 13.3, 4.0.
(iii) χ² = (50−47.2)²/47.2 + (30−35.4)²/35.4 + (15−13.3)²/13.3 + (5−4.0)²/4.0 = 0.16 + 0.83 + 0.22 + 0.23 = 1.44.
(iv) df = (4 − 1) − 1 (for the estimated λ) = 2. χ²_{0.05,2} = 5.991. Since 1.44 < 5.991, do not reject: the Poisson fits well. (The last expected count ≈ 4 < 5, so ideally combine the final cells.)

mark_scheme.point: df = 2 (−1 for estimated λ); do not reject

hint: Poisson MLE λ̂ = x̄; Fisher information I(λ) = n/λ so Var(λ̂) ≈ λ/n.
front:
Claim counts X₁,…,Xₙ are independent Poisson(λ).

(i) Write down the log-likelihood. **[1]**
(ii) Derive the maximum likelihood estimator of λ. **[2]**
(iii) State the approximate large-sample variance of λ̂. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** MLE recipe, then Fisher information for the variance.

**Solution:**
(i) P(x;λ) = e^(−λ)λ^x/x!. ℓ(λ) = −nλ + (Σxᵢ) ln λ − Σ ln(xᵢ!).
(ii) dℓ/dλ = −n + Σxᵢ/λ = 0 ⇒ λ̂ = Σxᵢ/n = x̄. The second derivative −Σxᵢ/λ² < 0 confirms a maximum.
(iii) Fisher information I(λ) = n/λ, so Var(λ̂) ≈ λ/n (estimated by x̄/n).

mark_scheme.point: Log-likelihood −nλ + Σx ln λ (− Σ ln x!)

hint: Gamma prior + Poisson data → Gamma posterior; mean α/β; Z = n/(β+n).
front:
The claim rate λ has a Gamma(α = 4, β = 2) prior (β the rate parameter). In one period 6 claims are observed.

(i) Identify the posterior distribution, justifying conjugacy. **[2]**
(ii) Give the posterior mean. **[1]**
(iii) Express the posterior mean as a credibility estimate and find Z. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** Gamma prior + Poisson likelihood → Gamma posterior; mean = α/β; credibility weight Z = n/(β + n).

**Solution:**
(i) Posterior ∝ λ^(α+Σx−1) e^(−(β+n)λ), a Gamma kernel, so the posterior is Gamma(α+Σx, β+n) = Gamma(4+6, 2+1) = Gamma(10, 3) (conjugate).
(ii) Posterior mean = 10/3 = 3.33.
(iii) Posterior mean = (α+Σx)/(β+n) = Z·(Σx/n) + (1−Z)·(α/β), with Z = n/(β+n) = 1/3 = 0.333. Check: 0.333·6 + 0.667·2 = 3.33 ✓.

hint: set.seed for reproducibility; replicate; sample(1:6, 10, replace = TRUE); a logical mean estimates the probability.
front:
(CS1B / R) Estimate, by simulation, the probability that the total of ten fair six-sided dice exceeds 40.

(i) Write reproducible R code to simulate 10000 such totals. **[4]**
(ii) Write code to estimate P(total > 40) and state what it approximates. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** `set.seed` for reproducibility; `replicate` to repeat the experiment; `sample(1:6, 10, replace = TRUE)` rolls ten dice; the mean of a logical vector estimates the probability.

**Solution:**
(i)
```r
set.seed(1)
totals <- replicate(10000, sum(sample(1:6, 10, replace = TRUE)))
```
(ii)
```r
mean(totals > 40)
```
This is the proportion of simulated totals exceeding 40, which approximates the true probability P(total > 40); the estimate improves as the number of simulations increases.
